lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Nancy Drew? Oh, mon Dieu, thank heavens. I wasn't sure anyone would come.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Ms. Delacroix? You called about a poisoning — and a ghost?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Both, ma chère. Roland Ashworth — the man demolishing my theater — collapsed last night. Doctors say he was poisoned. And before that... she appeared again.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Margaux Fontaine. The greatest actress who ever graced that stage. She died here in 1928 — on stage, during her final performance. And now her ghost walks the Monarch.

choices.text: Tell me about Margaux Fontaine.
What happened to Ashworth?
Who else is in the theater right now?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Margaux Fontaine was... how do I say this... she was luminous. She was performing 'The Crimson Veil' — her signature role — when she collapsed during the final act. Poison in the prop goblet, they said. Un accident terrible. But I never believed that. She was my godmother, Nancy. She held me as a baby. I still remember her perfume — jasmine and something warm. And I've spent my whole life in this theater, waiting for the truth.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You think she was murdered?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I know she was, ma chère. In my bones, I know. And I think her ghost knows it too.

choices.text: What happened to Ashworth?
Who else is in the theater right now?
I'll start investigating.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Roland Ashworth bought the Monarch six months ago. Plans to demolish it — mon beau théâtre — in three days. Luxury condominiums, he says. As if the world needs more of those. Last night, he was found unconscious in his office. Some kind of toxin — non-lethal, but très deliberate. He's recovering upstairs, furious and suspicious of everyone.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Does he have enemies here?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Everyone in this theater has a reason to want the demolition stopped. Myself included. I won't insult your intelligence by pretending otherwise.

choices.text: Tell me about Margaux Fontaine.
Who else is in the theater right now?
I'll start investigating.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: There's Edwin Hale — un historien, a theater historian. He's been documenting the Monarch for... oh, it must be fifteen years now. You'll find him in the auditorium. He practically lives in those seats. Stella Morrow is our stage manager. Practical woman — no nonsense, that one. Knows every inch of this building. She's backstage, as always.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: And young Diego Reyes — a playwright renting the projection booth. Un garçon charmant, but he's heard things at night he can't explain. It's rattled him, poor dear. Then there's Ashworth himself, recovering in the manager's office. He won't be happy to see a detective. He isn't happy about anything, vraiment.

choices.text: Tell me about Margaux Fontaine.
What happened to Ashworth?
I'll start investigating.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I left a master key at the concierge desk — it opens most doors in the theater. There's also a magnifying glass. It belonged to my late husband. He was... he had sharp eyes. You'll need them. And Nancy — sois prudente. Be careful. The Monarch has kept its secrets for over seventy years. It won't give them up easily.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Vivian, I found something in the dressing room. Margaux's diary.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Oh... oh, mon Dieu. I always hoped it survived. What does it say? Tell me — s'il te plaît.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She suspected she was being poisoned. Long before the final night. 'The tea tastes wrong again,' she wrote.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I knew it. I always knew it wasn't an accident.

choices.text: She mentions someone with the initials 'C.D.' — does that mean anything to you?
Tell me about the trunk in the dressing room.
Did Margaux have a lover? The diary mentions someone named 'J.'

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: C.D... Cecilia Drake. Margaux's understudy.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: She was young, ambitious, très talentueuse. Margaux adored her — took her under her wing. But Cecilia wanted more than mentorship. She wanted the role. She wanted... everything.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And after Margaux died—

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Cecilia took over Lady Ashford the very next night. The very next night, Nancy. She became a star. Built a forty-year career on the role she stole from a dead woman.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: She died in 1987. Celebrated. Beloved. While my Margaux was forgotten. C'est injuste. It's unbearable.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Margaux's trunk! I remember it from when I was small. She kept her most precious things inside.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: The combination was always a date — Margaux used dates she could never forget. She called it 'my last Halloween.' The night everything changed.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: October 31st...

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: 1928. The night of the final performance.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: J? I... oui, yes. Margaux had a great love. Un grand amour. A man named James — James Hale. He was a stagehand here at the Monarch.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Hale? As in Edwin Hale?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Edwin is James's grandson. That's why he's so... how shall I say... consumed by this theater, by Margaux's story. It's personal for him. His grandfather lost the love of his life on that stage.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That changes everything.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Nancy... promets-moi — promise me something. Whatever you find out — whoever is responsible, past or present — make sure Margaux's name is cleared. She deserves that much. She deserves everything.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Vivian, I found this in Margaux's trunk.

choices.text: Show her the locket.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: That's... c'est moi. That's me. I was four years old.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: 'To my little star.' Ma petite étoile. She called me that. Every time she held me. Every time.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: She kept it in the trunk. All these years, she... pardonne-moi... she locked it away because she knew. She knew she wouldn't be coming back.

choices.text: She wanted to protect what mattered most.
Vivian — do you know who killed her?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She wanted to protect what mattered most.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I've been waiting seventy years to know what happened to her. Soixante-dix ans. I've lived my whole life in this theater because I couldn't leave her behind.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: There are private archives in my office — Margaux's personal effects, letters, photographs. I've never let anyone touch them. Not once. Take whatever you need, ma chère.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Don't let this building come down without the truth coming out. Je t'en prie. Please.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I was four years old when she died. I remember the screaming. I remember someone carrying me out of the theater.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I've always suspected Cecilia. She was the understudy. She took the role. She never looked back. But I could never prove it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm going to find the proof. I promise.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: My office — there are private archives. Margaux's personal effects. Take whatever you need. Just bring me the truth.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Ah — you must be the detective Vivian called. Edwin Hale. Theater historian, Emerson College adjunct, author of — well, three books on New England theater history, if we're keeping track. Please, sit anywhere. The whole auditorium's empty, as usual.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Nancy Drew. How long have you been studying the Monarch?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Fifteen years. Well — fifteen years and four months, to be precise. I started in November of 2010. The Monarch is my life's work.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: This building isn't just bricks and velvet, Ms. Drew. It's a living archive. Every floorboard, every rivet, every scratch on these armrests has a story. And if Ashworth has his way, it'll all be rubble in exactly seventy-two hours.

choices.text: Tell me about The Crimson Veil.
What do you think about the ghost sightings?
Did you see anything suspicious last night?
Your grandfather was James Hale — Margaux's lover.
Stella says props have been going missing. Do you know anything about that?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Missing props? That's... concerning. Some of those pieces are historically significant — the Tiffany lamps alone date to 1923, and the candelabra is original to the opening gala.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Stella seems worried about it.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Stella has her — well, she has her reasons for worrying. But I wouldn't focus on missing props right now, Nancy. The poisoning, the ghost — those are the real mystery. The props are a — a separate matter entirely. A distraction.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You seem very sure they're separate. How would you know that unless you knew what was actually going on?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I — that's not what I — I'm a historian, Nancy. I know how to sort through noise to find the signal. That's literally my job. I'm not hiding anything, if that's what you're implying. I'm trying to help you prioritize.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I didn't say you were hiding anything.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: No. No, you didn't. I just — forget I said that. The answer to this mystery is on the stage, not on a prop shelf. I'd stake my reputation on it. What's left of it.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: The Crimson Veil was the jewel of the 1928 season — premiered September 15th, ran for six weeks. A gothic melodrama about a countess who discovers her husband has been poisoning her. The irony is... well, you'll see.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Margaux Fontaine was extraordinary in the role. Extraordinary. In the final act, her character drinks from a poisoned goblet. But on opening night — October 31st, 1928, a Thursday — the prop goblet contained real poison.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And it was ruled an accident?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: The official story — and I use that phrase with contempt — was that a stagehand made a terrible mistake. But the stagehand was never identified. Never named, never questioned. And Margaux's understudy, Cecilia Drake, took over the role the very next night and became a star. November 1st, 1928. I have the playbill.

choices.text: You think Cecilia Drake killed her?
I found Margaux's diary. She knew she was being poisoned.
Thank you, Edwin. I'll keep investigating.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You found her — wait. You found her diary? Where? In the dressing room?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...May I see it? Please. I've been looking for primary sources — firsthand accounts — about Margaux for fifteen years. This is... this is unprecedented.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She writes about feeling sick after tea, about a metallic taste. And she suspected someone specific.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Then she knew. She knew, and nobody listened. The police, the theater owners — they all called it an accident because it was easier.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: That diary could be the evidence we need. Don't lose it, Nancy.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I'm a historian, not a spiritualist. I deal in documents, not — not apparitions. But I've seen things here I can't explain. And I've tried, believe me.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Fog that rolls across the stage when no machine is running. A figure in white that appears under the ghost light and vanishes. The sound of a woman's voice reciting lines from The Crimson Veil — Act Three, Scene Four, to be specific. Word perfect.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Whether it's truly Margaux's spirit or something else entirely... this theater is not at peace. I can feel it. And I'm not a man who says things like that.

choices.text: The effects manual describes systems that could fake all of that.
Tell me about The Crimson Veil.
Interesting. I'll keep investigating.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You've read the — you've actually read the effects manual? The whole thing? That's... thorough.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Yes, theoretically, someone could use the old systems to stage a convincing ghost. Fog machines, the trapdoor — which is a Graves & Sons counterweight model, very reliable — proper lighting. It would be — it would be quite an undertaking.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: But they'd need extensive knowledge of the building. Months of preparation. They'd need to have — to have practiced. Extensively. Not that I've thought about it in detail. I'm just — speaking generally. As a historian.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Like saving the theater from demolition?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...That would certainly be a reason. Hypothetically speaking. But you'd want to — I mean, a person would want to look at all the suspects before jumping to conclusions. That's just good detective work, isn't it?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I was in my usual seat — row G, center — reviewing my notes. Chapter twelve revisions, if that matters. Around ten o'clock — well, closer to ten-fifteen, maybe ten-ten — I heard a crash from the manager's office.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's a fifteen-minute window you're not sure about.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I — I wasn't watching the clock. I was working. By the time I got there, Ashworth was on the floor. Stella called the ambulance. The rest of us waited. It was... unsettling.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Did you notice anything else? Anyone missing?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Everyone was — well, I can't say everyone was accounted for. It's a big building. Many hidden corners. Someone could have slipped away and come back without anyone noticing. Hypothetically.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You said 'hypothetically.'

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I said what I said. I'm trying to be helpful, Nancy. That's all.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I have my suspicions. Cecilia Drake had means, motive, and opportunity. She knew the prop table. She had access backstage. And she had everything to gain.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: But proving it after over seventy years? That's the challenge. If there's evidence, it's buried somewhere in this theater.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Then I'd better start digging.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Who told you — how did you—

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Vivian. She recognized the name.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I... yes. James Hale was my grandfather. He was a stagehand here in the twenties. He and Margaux... they were...

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: He never got over her death. Forty-three years, Nancy. He spent forty-three years insisting it wasn't an accident. Nobody believed him. He died in 1971 — March 14th — still fighting. Still writing letters to the police department.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I promised him I'd find the truth. I was twelve years old and I promised. That's why I'm here. That's why I've always been here.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's a heavy promise to carry for over fifty years.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Some promises are worth the weight.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: If you need anything — history, floor plans, old records, architectural details — I'm your man. I know this building better than anyone alive. Better than anyone who's ever lived, probably. Forgive the immodesty.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Oh, and Nancy? If you see the ghost... try talking to her. You never know. Stranger things have happened. Well — actually, have they? I'd need to check my notes.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Detective? Great. Fantastic. Another person poking around my backstage. I'm Stella Morrow — stage manager. Or I was, until Ashworth decided this place was worth more as a pile of expensive rubble.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You don't seem happy about the investigation.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I'm not happy about any of this. A man was poisoned, there's supposedly a ghost, I'm running on four hours of sleep and my third coffee, and in three days this whole building comes down. I just want to do my job and go home. Is that too much to ask? Apparently yes.

choices.text: Where were you when Ashworth collapsed?
What's it like working backstage here?
Have you noticed anything unusual lately?
I'll keep looking around.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I was right here, backstage, doing inventory. Because that's what I do. Every night. When I heard the crash I ran to the office and called 911. Check the call log if you want — 10:07 PM.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I didn't poison anyone. I don't even drink tea. Coffee. Black. Three cups minimum. That's my poison.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Eight years. Eight years of keeping this place running on spit and duct tape. I know every rope, every pulley, every creaky floorboard. The fly system alone takes two hours to balance properly — and nobody thanks you for it until something falls on the lead actor.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You must know this building inside out.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Better than anyone. Well — maybe Edwin would argue that. But I know the working parts. The mechanical parts. Edwin knows the history. There's a difference between reading about a trapdoor and actually operating one.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Not that it matters in three days when they bulldoze the whole thing.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Unusual? In this building? Where do I start?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Three months ago I found fresh footprints in old prop storage — a section nobody uses. Size eleven men's shoes. I mentioned it to Ashworth. He told me to 'stop being dramatic.' I'm a stage manager. Being dramatic is literally my job.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And more recently?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Noises. From below the stage. The kind of mechanical sounds this building hasn't made in decades. And the fog machine — I maintain it, I know exactly how much fluid is in it. Someone's been using it at night. I check the levels every morning. They don't lie.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: And props have been disappearing. Small things at first — a candlestick, a period-correct clock. Then bigger items. A Tiffany lamp from the balcony, gone. I keep a full inventory. Things don't just walk off.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Look, I don't know what's going on down there. But someone is using my equipment and taking my props without permission, and that bothers me more than any ghost.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: One more thing — there's a locked door at the back of backstage that leads to the basement. I don't have the key. Nobody's supposed to. But I've heard that door opening and closing at night.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Whatever's going on in this theater... it's coming from down there.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Stella, I found blueprints with hidden passages marked. Do you know about these?

choices.text: Show her the blueprints.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I knew about the passages — the effects manual mentions them in chapter six, I think. But I've never seen a complete map like this. Where did you even find this?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Okay, look — this passage connects the basement to the dressing room. And this one goes from behind the orchestra pit to the lobby. Someone could move through the entire building without ever being seen. That's... that's not great, actually.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone like our ghost.

choices.text: Have you ever been in the passages yourself?
Can you get me to the catwalk?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Once. Last month. 2 AM — I was here doing lighting inventory because apparently that's my life now. I heard footsteps behind the backstage wall. Told myself it was the building settling.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: But buildings don't settle in rhythm. Someone was walking back there. Steady, purposeful. They knew exactly where they were going.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone who's studied these blueprints.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: There's only one person who knows this theater well enough to navigate those passages in the dark. And he's been here every day for fifteen years. Do the math.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You need to get to the catwalk. I'll unlock the ladder — it's a pain, the lock sticks, but I'll deal with it. There's a lighting board up there. If someone's been running a light show, that's where they set it up.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Yeah. That's exactly what I was thinking. I'll unlock the ladder for you — fair warning, it's original 1920s metalwork and it creaks like a horror movie.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Thank you, Stella. You've been more helpful than you know.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Just be careful up there. I've already got one person poisoned and a ghost haunting the building — I don't need someone falling off a ninety-year-old catwalk on top of everything else. And whatever you find... remember that not everyone in this theater is a villain. Some of us are just trying to keep the lights on. Literally.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Oh! Whoa — sorry, didn't hear you come up. I was in the zone. You know how it is — 'the pen is the tongue of the mind,' as Cervantes said. Diego Reyes. I'm renting the projection booth as a writing studio.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Nancy Drew. I'm investigating the poisoning — and the ghost.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: The ghost is real. I mean — okay, I know how that sounds coming from a playwright, but I've heard her. At night, from up here, I can hear someone reciting lines on the empty stage. Act three of The Crimson Veil. Word for word. It's like something out of Phantom of the Opera, except scarier because it's actually happening.

choices.text: Have you found anything unusual up here?
What have you heard at night?
I decoded the script annotations — it spells 'goblet.'

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Actually, yes! I found an original script of The Crimson Veil stuffed inside a film canister. Handwritten annotations all through the margins in red ink. It's like finding a first folio in a dumpster — sorry, I'm a theater nerd.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I thought it was blocking notes at first, but the marks are weird. Circled letters that don't match any stage directions I know. It's like — you know in mystery novels when there's a cipher? It's exactly like that, except I'm terrible at ciphers.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: How many circled letters are there?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I counted six in Act III alone — scattered through the stage directions, not the dialogue. They don't spell anything obvious... at least, nothing I could figure out. But someone went through a lot of trouble to mark them.

choices.text: Can I take a closer look at it?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Be my guest. It's right here on my desk. I've been trying to crack it for weeks.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: You know what's weird? The annotations are only in Act III — the act where Lady Ashford drinks from the goblet. Whoever wrote these notes was focused on that specific scene.

choices.text: What have you heard at night?
Thanks, Diego. I'll look into this.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Goblet? As in — THE prop goblet from the play? The murder weapon?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The circled letters in order: G-O-B-L-E-T. Someone annotated the script to record that the goblet was the murder weapon.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Holy — okay, this is like Chekhov's gun except it's Margaux's goblet. Someone knew. Someone wrote it down in code in the margins of the actual script. And that script has been sitting in a film canister up here for over seventy years!

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The question is who wrote the annotations. And when.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: The ink is old — really old. That's not modern handwriting, trust me, I've studied enough old scripts. Whoever coded that message did it close to 1928. This is... this is the kind of thing that wins Pulitzers. Or gets people killed. Or both.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: It started about a month ago. I stay late — the booth is quiet, great for writing. You know, 'the midnight hour is close at hand' — sorry, that's Michael Jackson, not Shakespeare. Anyway, around midnight, I hear footsteps below. Then fog drifts across the stage.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Last week, I heard what sounded like machinery — grinding, mechanical sounds from under the stage. Like old gears turning. I almost called the cops, but what was I going to say? 'Hello, 911? The floor is angry?'

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: And then one night... I looked down from the booth window and saw a woman in white, standing center stage. She turned, looked up — right at me — and vanished. Through the floor. I'm not going to lie, Nancy, I screamed. Out loud. Like a character in a bad horror movie. It was not my finest moment.

choices.text: Through the floor? Like a trapdoor?
The effects manual describes a trapdoor system in the stage floor.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: That's... actually a really good point. I didn't think of it as a trapdoor because — well, ghosts don't need trapdoors. That's like, Haunting 101.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: But a person would. Oh man. I feel like the guy in the Scooby-Doo episode who doesn't realize it's Old Man Jenkins until the very end.

choices.text: Have you found anything unusual up here?
Thanks, Diego. I'll keep investigating.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Wait — you're saying the theater has an actual trapdoor in the stage? Like, a real one? That someone could use to pull a full Hamlet's Ghost?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The 1925 effects manual describes a counterweight system that can lift a person from the basement to center stage in seconds.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: So the ghost isn't floating through the floor. Someone is riding an elevator through it. That's... diabolically theatrical.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I feel so stupid. I'm a playwright. I literally write stage directions for a living. I should know about trapdoors. Shakespeare used trapdoors! Marlowe used trapdoors! What is wrong with me?

choices.text: Have you found anything unusual up here?
Thanks, Diego. I'll keep investigating.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Hey, one more thing — I've been writing a play about all this. 'The Ghost of the Monarch.' My agent says it's the best thing I've ever written, which is either exciting or a devastating commentary on everything I wrote before.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I hope the real ending is worth writing about too. Every good story deserves a third act, right?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Oh, wonderful. Another vulture. What do you want? I'm on a deadline — literally.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Nancy Drew. I'm here to find out who poisoned you, Mr. Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Ha. Take your pick. Everyone in this building wants me dead. The old actress, the historian, even my own stage manager. This theater is full of people who'd rather see me in the ground than see one brick moved.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: You want my opinion? Start with Hale. The man's been circling this building like a vulture for fifteen years. He's got keys he shouldn't have, he knows rooms that aren't on the blueprints, and he looks at me like I'm a war criminal for filing a demolition permit. Start with Hale.

choices.text: What happened the night you collapsed?
Someone marked up your blueprints. Was that you?
You mentioned seeing someone in the building at night.
Your wall safe contains insurance documents. The demolition is worth $2.3 million — you rejected an $800K offer to sell.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I was working late. Had a cup of tea from the kettle — same as every night. Earl Grey, two sugars, not that it matters. Within twenty minutes, my vision went sideways and I couldn't stand. I'm a big man, Drew. I don't just fall down.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Before I blacked out, I saw... someone. A shape in the doorway. Just standing there. Watching me fall.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Can you describe them?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Dark clothes. Average build. They didn't help. They didn't call out. They just watched, like I was — like I was a show. And then they were gone. I woke up on the floor with Stella shaking me.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Did anyone else see this figure?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: No one else was — I was alone in the office. But I know what I saw. I'm not making this up.

choices.text: I found the teacup. The residue looks like antimony — a metallic poison.
Can I see the blueprints on your wall?
I'll examine the office.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Antimony? That's — you're serious. That's a Victorian poison. Nobody uses that. Nobody even thinks about that unless they've got their nose in some dusty —

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone who's been researching a murder from 1928.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Hale. You're talking about Hale. Of course you are. That man treats a seventy-year-old crime scene like it's a personal hobby.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm not accusing anyone yet. But the connection between the compounds used on you and the ones used on Margaux Fontaine is significant.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Then stop not accusing and start acting. Get him out of my theater. I want him gone, I want his boxes gone, and I want this demolition back on schedule. Am I making myself clear?

choices.text: Someone marked up your blueprints. Was that you?
You mentioned seeing someone in the building at night.
That's all for now.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: How did you — that safe was locked. I changed the combination myself.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The Historical Society offered you $800,000 for the theater. You turned it down because the demolition insurance pays $2.3 million.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: That's a business decision. It's called math, Drew. Not a crime.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: It's a motive. If the theater gets landmark status, you can't demolish it. You lose the insurance payout and have to accept the lower offer. Everyone in this building wants to save the theater — and you have a $1.5 million reason to destroy it.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I'm the victim here. Someone poisoned ME. I was on a hospital floor for six hours. So don't you dare turn this around on me — I won't have it.

choices.text: What happened the night you collapsed?
That's all for now.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I didn't draw those red marks. Don't look at me like that — I didn't. They were already there when I pinned the blueprints up. Someone's been mapping hidden passages, sealed rooms, a whole tunnel network beneath the stage.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: This theater is a maze. Whoever built it wanted people to disappear. And apparently, someone still does.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Do you know who made the annotations?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: My money's on the historian. Hale. The man practically lives here — he knows more about this building than the people who built it. And that makes me nervous.

choices.text: Tell me about the intruder you found.
Thank you, Mr. Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Two weeks ago. I came in early — 5 AM, because the demolition crew needed measurements. I heard noises from below the stage. Mechanical sounds. Rhythmic, like machinery. The basement door was unlocked, which it shouldn't have been. I went down.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Someone had set up a workshop down there. Tools, documents, chemicals I couldn't identify. And then they heard me coming. The lights went out — all of them, at once. I couldn't see my hand in front of my face. I barely made it back to the stairs.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: That's when the 'ghost' sightings ramped up. Fog in the hallways, voices from the walls. And three days later — three days — I was poisoned. You tell me that's a coincidence.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You think they poisoned you to keep you quiet?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I don't think. I know. And I'll tell you something else — if you find whoever's down there, you'll find whoever tried to kill me. Count on it.

choices.text: What happened the night you collapsed?
Someone marked up your blueprints. Was that you?
That's all for now.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Find whoever did this. Fast. I'm not asking — I'm telling you. Because in seventy-two hours, my crew arrives and this building comes down. Permits are signed, inspections are done.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Ghost or no ghost. That's the deadline. Work with it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Edwin. I've solved it. Both cases — 1928 and today.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Both? You mean —

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Cecilia Drake murdered Margaux Fontaine. She swapped the prop goblet for a real one and poisoned the wine in Act III. Your grandfather was right.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: After all these years... someone else finally sees it. Someone who isn't a Hale, who doesn't have a reason to believe it. You just... followed the evidence.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And Ashworth poisoned himself.

choices.text: Ashworth staged the whole thing for the insurance payout.
Your grandfather was James Hale. This was always personal for you.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: He — what? Ashworth poisoned himself?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: A $400,000 hazard condemnation clause in his insurance policy. If the building was deemed dangerous — say, because of a poisoning — the payout doubles. He used a pharmaceutical-grade compound, carefully dosed. Non-lethal. Just dramatic enough to trigger an investigation.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: That's... that's monstrous. He turned a real crime scene — a place where a woman was actually murdered — into an insurance scheme. He was going to demolish the evidence of a ninety-seven-year-old murder for money.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And you were staging a ghost to stop him. Two men, two frauds, both convinced they were the hero of the story.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...When you put it like that, it sounds absurd. And I suppose it is. A historian and a developer, playing games in a dead woman's theater.

choices.text: The question is what happens now.
Was any of it worth it?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You found out about my grandfather.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: James Hale. He was a stagehand here. He loved Margaux. He spent his whole life trying to prove she was murdered.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: He died when I was twelve. November 3rd, 1986 — I remember the date because it snowed and grandfathers aren't supposed to die when it snows. The last thing he said to me was: 'Promise me you'll find the truth about Margaux.' I was a kid. I didn't understand.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: But I grew up. And fifteen years ago, I started keeping that promise. And now, because of you, it's finally done.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The ghost staging, the trespassing, the fraud — that was all you. But the poisoning was Ashworth's own scheme. An insurance payout.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: He poisoned himself? For money? While I was down here trying to save Margaux's legacy, he was turning her theater into a — a balance sheet?

choices.text: The question is what happens now.
Was any of it worth it?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I found the truth about a murder that went unsolved for ninety-seven years. I kept a promise my grandfather made to a dying woman. And I committed trespassing, property damage, and fraud to do it.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Was it worth it?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Ask me on a good day and I'll say yes. Ask me at three in the morning when I'm rehearsing ghost cues alone in a basement and I'll say I don't know anymore.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm asking you right now.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Right now I'm a fifty-three-year-old man standing in a basement, waiting for a detective half my age to decide my fate. Right now I'm terrified. And I'm ashamed. And I'd do it all again tomorrow if it meant Margaux's name wasn't buried under concrete.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: That's the truth. I wish it were simpler. I'm a historian — I'm supposed to make things simpler. But this one I can't.

choices.text: I'm calling the police. The ghost staging, the fraud — the law should sort this out.
Dad said proving a historical crime triggers emergency heritage review. Let's go public — all of it.
Walk away, Edwin. Disappear. I'll make sure Margaux's story gets told.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Honestly? I haven't thought that far ahead. Fifteen years of momentum and I don't know how to stop. I never planned for 'after.' Historians aren't supposed to become part of the story.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Part of me wants to turn myself in — the ghost staging, the trespassing, all of it. Part of me wants to run. And part of me wants to stand on that stage and tell the whole world what Cecilia Drake did and what Ashworth tried to cover up.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And what about what you did? The fraud. The people you scared.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I'll carry that either way. Whether I'm in a courtroom or a library in Scotland or standing in this theater — I'll carry it. I faked a ghost in the same building where a real woman died. The irony isn't lost on me. It's never lost on me.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Then the question is what happens to the Monarch. And to Margaux's story.

choices.text: I'm calling the police. The ghost staging, the fraud — the law should sort this out.
Dad said proving a historical crime triggers emergency heritage review. Let's go public — all of it.
Walk away, Edwin. Disappear. I'll make sure Margaux's story gets told.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I've been waiting for someone to say that. Isn't that strange? Part of me is... relieved. Genuinely relieved.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Edwin—

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: No, I mean it. I've been carrying this alone for months. The lying, the hiding, creeping through passages at 2 AM. I can't keep cataloging my own lies. I'm running out of filing space.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: And Ashworth — he should face charges too. Self-poisoning for an insurance payout is fraud. The Monarch deserves better than both of us.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Just — promise me one thing. Make sure Cecilia's letters get to the historical society. All forty-seven of them. Margaux deserves to be remembered for more than how she died.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I promise.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: My grandfather kept his promise for fifty years. You'll keep yours. I can tell — you have that same look he had. Stubborn. Certain.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Sometimes the truth isn't enough to save what we love. But it's always enough to matter.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Together? You'd — you'd actually do that? After everything I've —

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The world should know what happened to Margaux Fontaine. Cecilia's crime. Ashworth's fraud. And yes — your ghost. All of it.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I'll face whatever comes. The charges for the staging, the trespassing — I don't care. I've spent fifteen years in archives. A courtroom can't be worse.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The 1928 murder, the cover-up, Ashworth's insurance scheme, your grandfather's research — we put it all in front of the public and let them decide what this building means.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Vivian will be furious about the ghost. But maybe — maybe she'll understand why. She knew my grandfather. She knows what promises mean.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Then let's give the Monarch one last opening night. Not for a ghost — for the woman who deserved to be remembered.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You'd let me go? After what I — you can't be serious.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm letting Margaux's story be told. Ashworth's fraud will come out regardless. What happens to you... that's between you and your conscience.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: My conscience. Right. My well-organized, thoroughly documented, deeply guilty conscience.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Tell Vivian I'm sorry. For all of it. For hiding down here like a character in one of her old plays. For using Margaux's memory as a prop. For not trusting her enough to ask for help. Tell her I'm sorry I couldn't say goodbye.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Edwin.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Yes?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Your grandfather would be proud of what you found. Not how you found it. But what you found.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: That's the kindest lie anyone's ever told me. And I've read a lot of lies — professionally speaking.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I told myself it was the right call. I still tell myself that. Most days, I believe it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I pick up the heavy receiver and the ringing stops. An old rotary phone on the lobby wall — the theater's landline hasn't been disconnected yet.

choices.text: Answer the incoming call.
Call Bess — ask about Ashworth's company.
Call George — ask about county records.
Call Ned — ask about the poisoning.
Call Bess and George.
Call Dad.
Look up the local Historical Society.
Call Ned.
Try the Hardy boys' number.
Hang up.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone's already on the line — the phone was ringing when I picked it up.

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: Nancy? Oh good, you made it. Hannah said you left in a hurry this morning — something about a theater and a ghost?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Hi Dad. Yes, I'm at the Monarch Theatre. Vivian Delacroix called me — she thinks the ghost of an actress from 1928 is haunting the place. And someone was poisoned here last night.

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: Poisoned? Nancy, that changes things. Be very careful. If someone in that building is willing to use poison, they're dangerous.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I know, Dad. I'll be careful.

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: I mean it. Check in with me when you can. And Nancy — Vivian Delacroix's family has a complicated history with that theater. Her godmother was the actress who died on stage. There's more going on there than meets the eye.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's what I'm counting on. I'll call you when I know more.

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: You'd better. And eat something — I know how you get when you're on a case.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: Nancy! I've been digging into Ashworth's company — Monarch Properties LLC. And girl, it's shady.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: Three insurance claims in the last five years. All on properties he demolished. One was a warehouse that had a 'mysterious fire' two weeks before demo. Another was a school that got condemned for 'environmental contamination.' Sound familiar?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: A pattern of manufactured hazards to trigger insurance payouts.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: Exactly. Each time, the insurance payout was way more than the property was worth. He's not a developer, Nancy. He's a con artist with a demolition crew.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Bess, you might have just cracked this case wide open.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: I LIVE for this. Also, is Diego still there? George says I'm not allowed to ask but I'm asking anyway.

lines.speaker: George
lines.text: Hey, Nan. I pulled the county records on the Monarch Theatre like you asked.

lines.speaker: George
lines.text: Here's the interesting part — there was a building inspection six months ago. The inspector flagged structural concerns that should have delayed demolition by at least eight months. But the report was never filed with the county. It just... vanished.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone buried it.

lines.speaker: George
lines.text: Gets better. I cross-referenced the inspector's name with Ashworth's other properties. Same inspector signed off on two of his previous demolitions — both times after similar reports 'went missing.' The inspector's name is M. Lawson.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: M. Lawson. I've seen that name before.

lines.speaker: George
lines.text: Thought you might have. Also — the sealed inspection from six months ago? It rated the theater as structurally sound. The building isn't in danger. Someone is manufacturing the justification to tear it down.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: Nancy? Hey. You okay? You sounded shaken on your voicemail last night.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm fine, Ned. But I need your help with something. Can you look up antimony poisoning for me?

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: Antimony? Sure, give me a sec... okay. Antimony potassium tartrate — also called tartar emetic. It's a metallic compound. Used historically in medicine, also as a poison. Symptoms include nausea, vomiting, cardiac irregularities.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: Here's the thing, Nan — the lethal dose is really specific. For a man Ashworth's size, you'd need about 300 milligrams to be dangerous. But a non-lethal dose — one that makes you sick but doesn't kill you — is almost harder to calculate. Whoever did this knew exactly how much to use.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Or they were doing it to themselves and wanted to make sure they'd survive.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: ...Whoa. That's dark, Nan. But yeah — if someone wanted to fake a poisoning convincingly without actually dying, knowing the exact dosage would be critical. And that's way easier to calibrate when you're the one taking it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Thank you, Ned. That's exactly what I needed to hear.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: Be careful in there. And call me if you need anything. I mean it — anything.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: Nancy! Oh my gosh, finally! George and I have been going down the biggest rabbit hole — okay, so the Monarch Theatre was designed by the same architect who built the Ryerson Opera House. He was obsessed with hidden passages. Like, obsessed obsessed. There are blueprints online and half the walls have secret compartments!

lines.speaker: George
lines.text: What Bess is trying to say, between exclamation points, is that the building probably has more hidden rooms than visible ones. I also ran some searches on Cecilia Drake. She had a long career after 1928 — three theater awards, two memoirs, dozens of interviews. And she never once mentioned Margaux Fontaine. Not once in forty years.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: That's suspicious, right? If your mentor died tragically and you took over her role, you'd at least mention her! Unless you had a really, really good reason not to.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Definitely suspicious. Oh — the stage tech here, Diego, has been really helpful. He knows the theater inside and out.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: Diego? Is he cute? Wait — how old is he? What does he look like?

lines.speaker: George
lines.text: Bess. She's investigating a poisoning.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: What? Multitasking is a valid investigative skill! Nancy, you can solve crimes AND notice if someone has nice eyes. I believe in you.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'll call back when I know more. About the case, Bess. The case.

lines.speaker: Bess
lines.text: Fine, fine. But I want details. ALL the details.

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: Nancy! I was just thinking about you. Are you eating? The last time you called from a case, you'd been living on vending machine peanuts for two days.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm fine, Dad. But the case is complicated. There's a modern poisoning connected to a possible murder from 1928. And the theater is being demolished in three days.

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: Hmm. Well, the statute of limitations on a 1928 murder expired decades ago — you can't prosecute Cecilia Drake even posthumously. But here's the interesting thing. If the evidence proves murder, it could qualify the theater for landmark protection under the Cultural Heritage Act. Section 14(b), if I'm remembering correctly.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: So proving Margaux was murdered could actually save the theater?

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: If the evidence is strong enough, yes. A site of a documented historical crime qualifies for emergency heritage review. That would legally halt any demolition until the review is complete. It's not a permanent solution, but it buys time.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Thanks, Dad. That changes everything.

lines.speaker: Carson Drew
lines.text: Just be careful, sweetheart. Old theaters and old secrets — neither one is as stable as it looks. And eat something.

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: River Heights Historical Society, how may I help you?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm calling about the Monarch Theatre landmark petition.

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: Ah yes — Mr. Hale's petition. It's been in review for three months. The problem is, we need evidence of significant historical events at the site. The architecture alone isn't enough.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: What if I could prove that a murder took place there in 1928?

lines.speaker: Receptionist
lines.text: That would certainly qualify. If you can provide documented evidence, we can file for an emergency heritage review. That would legally halt any demolition until the review is complete.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I may have exactly what you need. I'll be in touch.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: Nancy! I was hoping you'd call. I've been staring at my phone between classes like a weirdo. How's the haunted theater?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Complicated. There's a murder from 1928, a modern poisoning, a fake ghost, and the building might be demolished in three days.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: So... a normal Tuesday for you. My biggest problem today was a jammed stapler, so clearly our lives are equally exciting.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'd trade you for the stapler right about now.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: No you wouldn't. You love this stuff. Just promise me you'll be careful? Old buildings have a way of falling apart at the worst possible moment. And I'd really like my girlfriend back in one piece.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I always am. Well, mostly.

lines.speaker: Ned
lines.text: 'Mostly' is exactly the word that keeps me up at night. Call me when you solve it — I know you will.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I dial a number I've had memorized for years. It rings twice, then clicks to voicemail.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: 'You've reached Frank and Joe Hardy. We're probably on a case. Leave a message and we'll call you back — unless we're tied up. Literally. Again.' *beep*

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Hey boys, it's Nancy. I'm at the Monarch Theatre in River Heights — haunted theater, 1928 murder, poisoning, secret passages, the whole package. Honestly, it's the kind of case you two would fight over. If you're ever in town, I could use a second opinion. Or a third. But no rush — I'll probably have it solved by the time you check your messages. Talk soon.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I set the receiver back in its cradle. The phone clicks and goes silent.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Nancy! You came back. I wasn't sure you would — after what happened last night. Mon Dieu, when I heard you saw her too... I barely slept.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That makes two of us. I have more questions, Vivian. A lot more.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Of course you do. Sit, sit. I was just making tea — oh, don't give me that look, it's chamomile, perfectly safe. I made it myself. Although I suppose after yesterday, none of us should trust anything served in this theater, non?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I'm joking, ma chère. Mostly. You look like you haven't eaten. There are biscuits in the tin — take two, you'll need the energy.

choices.text: Vivian, I found Margaux's diary in the dressing room.
I found something in Margaux's trunk. I think it's yours.
The mirror in Margaux's dressing room — she wrote 'C.D.' on it. Does that mean anything to you?
How are you holding up?
Tell me more about the theater's history.
Have you noticed anything strange lately?
I should keep investigating.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: How am I holding up? Oh, you know. The building I've lived in for seventy-three years is being demolished. There's a ghost that may or may not be my godmother. A man was poisoned upstairs. And a teenager is my best hope.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm not a teenager anymore, Vivian.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Everyone under fifty is a teenager to me, darling. It's one of the few privileges of old age — along with saying exactly what you think and pretending you can't hear things you don't want to.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: But truly? I'm frightened. Not of the ghost — I've lived with Margaux's memory my whole life. I'm frightened of what happens when the wrecking ball comes. Where does an old actress go when her stage is gone?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: We'll figure this out, Vivian. I promise.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: You remind me of her, you know. Margaux. She had that same stubborn certainty. It's either your greatest strength or a terrible liability. I could never decide which it was with her.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: The Monarch opened in 1924 — did you know it was almost called 'The Imperial'? But the original owner, Theodore Whitfield, thought that sounded too pretentious. Can you imagine? A man who built a theater with gold leaf ceilings and marble pillars worrying about pretension.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Opening night was Hamlet. Terrible production, by all accounts. The lead actor forgot the 'To be or not to be' soliloquy and improvised something about the weather. Theodore fired him during intermission.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's... quite a debut.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: The theater has always attracted drama, ma chère. Both kinds. You're standing in a building where Sarah Bernhardt once dropped her glass eye into the orchestra pit and made a stagehand fish it out during the second act.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I miss those days. The theater was alive then. People came here to feel something. Now they stare at their telephones and call it entertainment. Bah.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Strange? Ma chère, I live in a haunted theater where a man was poisoned and a detective is interrogating everyone. You'll have to be more specific about 'strange.'

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Fair point. Anything out of the ordinary — for you?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Hmm. The lights in the auditorium flicker sometimes. Always three times, then they stop. Edwin says it's the wiring. I say wiring doesn't have a sense of rhythm.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: And I keep finding fresh flowers on the stage. White gardenias — Margaux's favorite. Someone is leaving them. Not me. I'm too old to climb those stairs for a bouquet.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: White gardenias. Interesting.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: You detectives and your 'interesting.' My husband used to say that too. Right before he solved something clever. Go on — I can see you thinking.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Go, go. Solve the mystery. Save my theater. And Nancy — don't skip meals. You detectives always forget to eat. There are crackers in the concession stand. They're only slightly stale.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Oh... oh, mon Dieu. I always hoped it survived. What does it say? Tell me — s'il te plaît.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She suspected she was being poisoned. Long before the final night. 'The tea tastes wrong again,' she wrote.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I knew it. I always knew it wasn't an accident.

choices.text: She mentions someone with the initials 'C.D.' — does that mean anything to you?
Did Margaux have a lover? The diary mentions someone named 'J.'
I'll keep reading. There might be more.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: C.D... Cecilia Drake. Margaux's understudy.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: She was young, ambitious, très talentueuse. Margaux adored her — took her under her wing. But Cecilia wanted more than mentorship. She wanted the role. She wanted... everything.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And after Margaux died—

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Cecilia took over Lady Ashford the very next night. The very next night, Nancy. She became a star. Built a forty-year career on the role she stole from a dead woman.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: She died in 1987. Celebrated. Beloved. While my Margaux was forgotten. C'est injuste. It's unbearable.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: J? I... oui, yes. Margaux had a great love. Un grand amour. A man named James — James Hale. He was a stagehand here at the Monarch.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Hale? As in Edwin Hale?

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Edwin is James's grandson. That's why he's so... how shall I say... consumed by this theater, by Margaux's story. It's personal for him. His grandfather lost the love of his life on that stage.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That changes everything.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: That's... c'est moi. That's me. I was four years old.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: 'To my little star.' Ma petite étoile. She called me that. Every time she held me. Every time.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: She kept it in the trunk. All these years, she... pardonne-moi... she locked it away because she knew. She knew she wouldn't be coming back.

choices.text: She wanted to protect what mattered most.
Vivian — do you know who killed her?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She wanted to protect what mattered most.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I've been waiting seventy years to know what happened to her. Soixante-dix ans. I've lived my whole life in this theater because I couldn't leave her behind.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: There are private archives in my office — Margaux's personal effects, letters, photographs. I've never let anyone touch them. Not once. Take whatever you need, ma chère.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Don't let this building come down without the truth coming out. Je t'en prie. Please.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I was four years old when she died. I remember the screaming. I remember someone carrying me out of the theater.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: I've always suspected Cecilia. She was the understudy. She took the role. She never looked back. But I could never prove it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm going to find the proof. I promise.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: My office — there are private archives. Margaux's personal effects. Take whatever you need. Just bring me the truth.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: C.D.... Margaux wrote that? On her own mirror?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: In wax, hidden under the surface. She knew who was poisoning her and she left an accusation where only someone looking carefully would find it.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Cecilia Drake. She — my godmother wrote the killer's initials on her mirror and nobody found them for seventy years. Mon Dieu. She was crying out for help and nobody heard her.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I heard her, Vivian. Seventy years late, but I heard her.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Then make it count, Nancy. Make it count.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Ms. Drew. Two nights in a row. I must say, I didn't expect you to come back after... well. After what you saw last night. Most people would have called the police. Or a priest.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm not most people. And what I saw wasn't a ghost, Edwin.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: No? Fascinating. You sound very certain for someone who watched a dead woman materialize on stage. But please — questions. I've had all day to think, and I suspect you have too.

choices.text: Your grandfather was James Hale — Margaux's lover.
I found Margaux's diary. She knew she was being poisoned.
Stella says props have been going missing. Do you know anything about that?
The effects manual describes systems that could fake all of that.
What are you working on?
You seem to know this place better than anyone.
Have you been sleeping here?
Have you been in the basement recently?
I'll let you get back to your research.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...Who told you — how did you—

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Vivian. She recognized the name.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I... yes. James Hale was my grandfather. He was a stagehand here in the twenties. He and Margaux... they were...

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: He never got over her death. Forty-three years, Nancy. He spent forty-three years insisting it wasn't an accident. Nobody believed him. He died in 1971 — March 14th — still fighting. Still writing letters to the police department.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I promised him I'd find the truth. I was twelve years old and I promised. That's why I'm here. That's why I've always been here.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's a heavy promise to carry for over fifty years.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Some promises are worth the weight.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You found her — wait. You found her diary? Where? In the dressing room?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...May I see it? Please. I've been looking for primary sources — firsthand accounts — about Margaux for fifteen years. This is... this is unprecedented.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She writes about feeling sick after tea, about a metallic taste. And she suspected someone specific.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Then she knew. She knew, and nobody listened. The police, the theater owners — they all called it an accident because it was easier.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: That diary could be the evidence we need. Don't lose it, Nancy.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Missing props? That's... concerning. Some of those pieces are historically significant — the Tiffany lamps alone date to 1923, and the candelabra is original to the opening gala.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Stella seems worried about it.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Stella has her — well, she has her reasons for worrying. But I wouldn't focus on missing props right now, Nancy. The poisoning, the ghost — those are the real mystery. The props are a — a separate matter entirely. A distraction.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You seem very sure they're separate. How would you know that unless you knew what was actually going on?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I — that's not what I — I'm a historian, Nancy. I know how to sort through noise to find the signal. That's literally my job. I'm not hiding anything, if that's what you're implying. I'm trying to help you prioritize.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I didn't say you were hiding anything.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: No. No, you didn't. I just — forget I said that. The answer to this mystery is on the stage, not on a prop shelf. I'd stake my reputation on it. What's left of it.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You've read the — you've actually read the effects manual? The whole thing? That's... thorough.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Yes, theoretically, someone could use the old systems to stage a convincing ghost. Fog machines, the trapdoor — which is a Graves & Sons counterweight model, very reliable — proper lighting. It would be — it would be quite an undertaking.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: But they'd need extensive knowledge of the building. Months of preparation. They'd need to have — to have practiced. Extensively. Not that I've thought about it in detail. I'm just — speaking generally. As a historian.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Like saving the theater from demolition?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: ...That would certainly be a reason. Hypothetically speaking. But you'd want to — I mean, a person would want to look at all the suspects before jumping to conclusions. That's just good detective work, isn't it?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I'm working on my fourth book, actually. 'Ghosts of the Footlights: Architecture, Memory, and the Haunted Stage.' The working title. My publisher thinks it's too long. My publisher thinks everything is too long. He wanted my last book to be 200 pages. It was 847.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's... thorough.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Thoroughness is underappreciated, Ms. Drew. Do you know that there are exactly 1,247 rivets in the balcony railing? I counted them. Twice. The second time I got 1,248, which kept me up for three nights until I found the extra one. It was hidden behind a renovation patch from 1956.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: My ex-wife used to say I care more about dead theaters than living people. She wasn't wrong, necessarily. But she was unkind about it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I think the theater is lucky to have someone who cares this much.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: That's... thank you. Most people just think I'm peculiar. Which I am. But it's nice to hear the alternative interpretation.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I know things about this building that the blueprints don't. For instance — did you notice the asymmetry in the lobby molding? The east wall has seventeen rosettes. The west wall has sixteen. One was removed in 1931 to patch a bullet hole.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: A bullet hole?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: A critic shot at the lead actor during a particularly bad production of 'The Tempest.' Missed entirely. Hit the wall. The management decided it was easier to cover it up than explain. Theater people.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I also know that the basement has a room that doesn't appear on any official plan. I found references to it in the original architect's personal correspondence. He called it 'the confession booth.' I've never found it, though. It haunts me. Figuratively. We already have enough literal haunting.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Sleeping here? I — well. Define 'sleeping.' If you mean 'occasionally dozing in seat G-12 between 2 and 5 AM,' then... perhaps.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Edwin.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Fine. Yes. I've been staying late. Very late. The acoustics change at night, you know — the building settles, the wood contracts, and you can hear things you'd never notice during the day. Whispers in the walls. Probably just pipes.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Probably.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's not exactly reassuring.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I find it comforting, actually. A building that still makes noise is a building that's still alive. It's when the Monarch goes silent that I'll worry. That'll mean Ashworth's won.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: The basement? No. No, I haven't been down there in years. Not since they sealed it off — the door has been locked since the renovation in 2019. I wouldn't even know how to get down there now.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Really? Because someone has been using the trapdoor mechanism. The fog machine controls are down there. And Stella says she's heard footsteps at 2 AM.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Stella says a lot of things. She's — look, I'm a historian, not a spelunker. I study documents. Blueprints. I don't go crawling around in basements in the middle of the night.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: If someone has been down there, that's — that's concerning. But it isn't me. I can assure you of that.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Right. Back to the rivets. If you need anything, I'll be here. I'm always here. That's either dedication or pathology, depending on who you ask.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Oh good, you came back. I half-expected you to bail after last night. Most of the crew won't even set foot in this building anymore — I had two stagehands quit over text this morning.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Because of the ghost?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Because of the 'ghost.' Yeah. Air quotes. You and I both know fog machines don't run themselves. Hold this — sandbag counterweight, don't drop it — I'm rebalancing the fly system since apparently I'm the only person left in this building who actually works.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Questions. Go. I've had a whole day to stew and I'm ready to talk.

choices.text: I found your note to the basement intruder — 'Stop now or I go to the police.'
Edwin told me about The Crimson Veil — could someone fake a ghost using the old systems?
I found your lockbox. $4,200 in sold props — is that for your mother's medical bills?
I saw the ghost, Stella. Someone's using your equipment to stage it.
Who were you on the phone with, Stella?
You seem stressed.
Tell me about working backstage.
I found blueprints with hidden passages marked. Do you know about these?
Is there anything else I should know?
I'll come back later.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Where did you find that? That was — I put that under —

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: On the stage floor. 'Stop now or I go to the police — S.M.' You wrote it.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: It could be anyone's initials. Sandra Mitchell, Steve — no. Fine. I wrote it. Three days ago. Slipped it under the basement door. Whoever's down there never responded.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You know who's in the basement.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I don't — I'm not sure. I have suspicions. But I need to know something first. Are you going to report what I've done? The props?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm interested in the truth, Stella. All of it.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: The truth. Right. Because that always works out so well for the person telling it.

choices.text: Tell me who's in the basement and I'll focus on the poisoning.
Let's talk about something else.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You saw — okay. Sit down. And don't drop that sandbag.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I've been going back and forth about telling you this for days. Losing sleep. Which is saying something, because I was already running on fumes.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone is using the fog machines, the trapdoor, and the lighting rig to stage a ghost. Professional-grade work.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I know. Because there's only one person in this building with the knowledge and the obsession to pull that off. And it's not me.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Edwin.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I've seen him going down to the basement at night. Two AM, three AM — always through the backstage door. I hear the trapdoor mechanism warming up. The counterweights shifting. Then fog rolls across the stage and the show starts.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I left him a note under the basement door — 'Stop now or I go to the police.' He never responded. Never even acknowledged it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Why didn't you say anything sooner?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Because part of me wanted it to work, okay? If a ghost story could get this building landmark status, could stop the wrecking ball — who was I to ruin that? This theater is the only thing I've got left.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: But then Ashworth got poisoned. And I don't know if Edwin did that too or not — honestly, it doesn't feel like him. But something is very wrong in this building and it's bigger than a fake ghost. You need to get down to that basement.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Edwin told you about The Crimson Veil? Of course he did. You can't say hello to that man without getting a forty-minute lecture on 1928.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Could someone fake a ghost? Yes. Easily. I could do it in my sleep. The fog machines still work. The trapdoor is original and functional. The lighting board on the catwalk can create any effect you want.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You sound like you've thought about it.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I'm a stage manager. It's literally my job to know how everything in this building works. I've been doing this for eight years — I could run a haunting with my eyes closed. But if you're asking whether I staged a ghost — no. God, no. I have enough problems without adding 'theatrical necromancy' to my résumé.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Who else would know the systems well enough?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Edwin. Full stop. He knows this building better than I do, and I hate admitting that. Fifteen years of studying every passage, every mechanism, every hidden corner. If anyone could make a convincing ghost... look, just — talk to Edwin.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You opened my — that was locked. How did you even — you know what, it doesn't matter.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I didn't do anything wrong. I didn't —

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Fine. Yes. My mother has stage four lymphoma. The bills are — you want a number? $47,000 and counting. So yes. I sold some props. Props that were going to be smashed into dust in three days anyway. At least this way someone — at least my mom gets something out of this disaster.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm not here about the props, Stella. I'm here about the poisoning.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Then why are you opening my lockbox? Why are you digging through my —

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: No. You know what. Fine. You want the truth? I know who's been in the basement. I've known for weeks. I wrote him a note. He ignored it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Who?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Edwin. It's always been Edwin.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I've seen him going down there at night. I know the sounds — the trapdoor mechanism, the fog machine warming up. He's been staging the ghost.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I didn't tell anyone because... honestly? I wanted the theater saved too. If a ghost story kept the demolition from happening, I wasn't going to stop it.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: But then Ashworth got poisoned, and everything changed. I don't think Edwin did that — the ghost staging is one thing, but poison? That's not him. Still, someone in this building crossed a line. I left Edwin a note warning him to stop. He never responded.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Where's the basement key?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Behind the lighting panel. Edwin thinks he's the only one who knows, but I've been stage manager here for eight years. I know every hiding spot in this building.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You — how did you — were you listening?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I heard enough. 'She doesn't know about Edwin.' You've been covering for him.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I wasn't covering, I was — okay. Fine. Yes. I know what Edwin's been doing in the basement. I've known for three weeks.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: That was my sister on the phone. She's a paralegal. I asked her what happens if someone gets caught staging a haunting in a building they don't own — trespassing, property damage, fraud. She said it's bad, Nancy. Really bad.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: So you were trying to protect Edwin?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I was trying to protect the theater. If Edwin gets arrested, the landmark petition dies. If the petition dies, Ashworth demolishes everything in three days. I was buying time.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: But the poisoning changed everything. I can't protect someone who — who might have —

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You don't think Edwin poisoned Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I don't know what I think anymore. I just know I'm tired of lying about it. Ask me what you want to ask me.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Stressed? No. This is my relaxed face. My stressed face involves more swearing and fewer sandbags.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You want to know what stress is? Stress is when the lead actress gets food poisoning twenty minutes before curtain and you have to convince the understudy — who has stage fright — that she's 'totally ready' while simultaneously fixing a broken spotlight with a rubber band and a prayer.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Did that actually happen?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Twice. Different actresses. Same understudy. She threw up both times. On stage. During the death scene. The audience thought it was method acting. We got a standing ovation.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Point is — a little poisoning and a ghost? That's a Tuesday.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You have a very unusual definition of 'Tuesday.'

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Welcome to theater, detective.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Backstage is where the real show happens. The audience sees the magic. I see the duct tape holding the magic together.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: See that rope over there? That's the main curtain line. Pull it wrong and you drop 300 pounds of velvet on whoever's standing center stage. I've been tempted. More than once. But I'm a professional.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Who tempted you?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Oh, every actor who's ever walked through those doors. There was one — Lord, what was his name — he demanded that his dressing room be repainted mid-run because the shade of blue 'conflicted with his aura.' His aura. I repainted it. I used the exact same shade. He said it felt 'much better.' I never told him.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: The point is, I know every trick, every trap, every hidden corner of this place. If someone's faking a ghost, they're using my equipment. And that offends me on a professional level.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You want to know something? Fine. Here's something nobody talks about.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Three months ago, before any of this ghost business, I found footprints in the dust backstage. In a section nobody uses — the old prop storage behind the scene dock. Fresh footprints.
