Size eleven men's shoes. I mentioned it to Ashworth. He told me to 'stop being dramatic.' I'm a stage manager. Being dramatic is literally my job.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Size eleven. Did you—

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I measured. Of course I measured. I also took a photo, which is on my phone, which I will show you if you ask nicely. I don't do things halfway, Drew. Unlike some people in this building who shall remain nameless but whose initials are R.A.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Where did you get — let me see those.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I knew about the passages — the effects manual mentions them in chapter six. But I've never seen a complete map like this. Someone drew these annotations by hand. Recently.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The markings show connections between the basement, the dressing room, the orchestra pit, and the lobby. Someone could move through the entire building without being seen.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Someone like our ghost.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Okay, look — this passage connects the basement to the dressing room. And this one goes from behind the orchestra pit to the lobby. Whoever mapped these knew exactly what they were doing.

choices.text: Have you ever been in the passages yourself?
Can you get me to the catwalk? If someone's been running a light show, that's where they set it up.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Once. Last month. Two AM — I was here doing lighting inventory because apparently that's my life now. I heard footsteps behind the backstage wall. Told myself it was the building settling.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: But buildings don't settle in rhythm. Someone was walking back there. Steady, purposeful. They knew exactly where they were going.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone who's studied these blueprints.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: There's only one person who knows this theater well enough to navigate those passages in the dark. And he's been here every day for fifteen years. Do the math.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You need to get to the catwalk. I'll unlock the ladder — the lock sticks, but I'll deal with it. There's a lighting board up there. If someone's been running a light show, that's where they programmed it.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: The catwalk. Yeah, that's exactly what I was thinking.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I'll unlock the ladder for you — fair warning, it's original 1920s metalwork and it creaks like a horror movie. But the lighting board up there controls everything — fog cues, spot sequences, the whole show.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: If someone programmed a ghost performance, the evidence would be up there.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Just be careful. I've already got one person poisoned and a ghost haunting the building — I don't need someone falling off a ninety-year-old catwalk. And whatever you find... remember that not everyone in this theater is a villain. Some of us are just trying to keep the lights on. Literally.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Fine. Go detect. Leave the sandbag on the bench. And if you see anyone touching my fly system, you have my permission to yell at them. Loudly.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Nancy! You actually came back! After last night I thought — well, I wrote three different scenes about it already. 'The detective returns to the scene of the haunting, drawn by something she can't name.' Too on the nose?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Did you see anything last night after I left?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I heard it. From up here — the lights surging, then nothing. Dead silence for about ten seconds. Then the front door slammed. I assumed that was you leaving. I didn't sleep, Nancy. I sat up here with every light on, writing. Twelve pages. Mostly garbage. But a couple of lines... I think I finally understand what fear does to a story.

choices.text: How's the play coming along?
Have you heard anything else at night?
What do you think about the other people here?
Diego, I found a chemical receipt in the coat check. Have you seen anything like that?
I'll let you get back to writing.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: The coat check receipt? Yeah — I found that two days ago. I've been staring at it trying to figure out who it belongs to.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: ChemSource Industrial Supply. The account name is 'Monarch Properties LLC.' I looked it up — it's a shell company. No website, no employees, just a P.O. box.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Monarch Properties LLC. That name is interesting.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: It gets more interesting. I found a crumpled receipt in Ashworth's trash yesterday — same company. Same account name. He orders takeout, leaves the containers everywhere. But he also left this.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Wait — you went through Ashworth's trash?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I'm a writer, Nancy. Observing people's garbage is basically a professional requirement. Hemingway used to —

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Focus, Diego.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Right. So. Same company. Same shell corporation. The coat check receipt lists antimony potassium tartrate — that's the poison, right? And Ashworth's receipt has... antimony potassium tartrate. Same compound. Same source.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: He ordered the poison that was used on him.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: That doesn't make sense. Unless...

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Unless it makes perfect sense.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: ...Oh. Oh no. Nancy, are you saying he—

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'm not saying anything yet. But I need you to keep this between us.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: This is going in the play. You can't stop me. This is the best twist I've ever — okay, okay, between us. For now. But Nancy, if Ashworth poisoned himself... why?

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's what I'm going to find out.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: It's... evolving. I came here to write a comedy about a playwright who moves into an old theater. Then the ghost started showing up and I accidentally switched genres. Now it's a psychological thriller. My agent is going to kill me.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: But honestly? The material is incredible. A real ghost — or whatever it is — real suspects, real danger. Chekhov said 'if there's a gun on the wall in act one, it has to go off in act three.' Well, this building has about forty guns on the wall and I'm terrified of act three.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Maybe don't put real crimes in your play.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Agatha Christie did it. Shakespeare did it. Truman Capote did it and he got invited to all the best parties afterward. I'm just continuing a grand literary tradition of profiting from other people's misery.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: That sounded worse out loud than it did in my head. I'm going to write it anyway.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Every. Single. Night. Last night it was Act Three, Scene Two — the part where Margaux's character discovers the poisoned wine. Whoever is doing this has impeccable diction. Like, genuinely better than most actors I've worked with.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You're critiquing the ghost's acting?

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I can't help it! I'm a playwright! It's like asking a chef not to judge someone's cooking. The phrasing, the breath control — it's almost too good. Real ghosts wouldn't have that kind of vocal discipline. That takes years of training.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Unless ghosts take acting classes in the afterlife. Which, honestly? I'd believe it. Actors never stop performing. Death is just an extended intermission for them.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's a pretty good line.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: I know. I'm writing it down. Don't tell anyone I got my best material from a detective.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Oh man. Okay. Off the record? Because if any of them finds out I said this, I'll deny everything.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Vivian is the most elegantly terrifying woman I've ever met. She can insult you in three languages without raising her voice. I once sneezed too loudly and she looked at me like I'd committed a war crime. I love her. She's a character in my play now. I haven't told her.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Edwin is... you know how dogs get when they're obsessed with a squirrel? That focused, single-minded intensity? That's Edwin, except the squirrel is a dead actress and the yard is a ninety-year-old crime scene. He scares me a little, honestly.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Stella is the only competent person in this building and she knows it. She once fixed a broken spotlight with a bobby pin and a piece of chewing gum. I saw her do it. She didn't even look up.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: And Ashworth? Ashworth is the kind of villain who doesn't know he's a villain. He genuinely thinks he's the hero of this story. 'I'm bringing progress!' Progress. To a building that's more interesting than anything he'll ever build on top of it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You've given this a lot of thought.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Writers observe. It's what we do. Also I have no social life and this is the most exciting thing that's ever happened to me, so.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Go forth and detect. I'll be here, turning your investigation into a two-act play. Don't worry — I'll change the names. Nancy Drew becomes... Fancy Brew. A coffee-obsessed detective. Totally different character.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: You again. I was hoping last night's little light show would've scared you off. It certainly scared off half my demolition crew — they're threatening hazard pay now. Hazard pay! For a building with a fog machine!

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I need a few more minutes of your time, Mr. Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Of course you do. Everyone just needs 'a few more minutes.' The Historical Society needed a few more minutes. My lawyer called six times today — six! And my mother saw 'something about a ghost' on the internet and wants to know if I'm eating enough fiber. As if fiber prevents hauntings.

choices.text: How are you feeling?
Why are you really demolishing this place?
Do you have any enemies?
Your insurance pays $2.3 million if the building is condemned. That's a lot of money.
I'll leave you to your calls.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Where did you hear — who told you about the insurance? That's confidential business information.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Public records, Mr. Ashworth. Your company has filed three insurance claims on demolished properties in five years.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: That's — those are completely separate — you're conflating — look. Business insurance is standard practice. Every developer carries it. Every single one.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: And the building inspector, M. Lawson? The same inspector who signed off on your last two demolitions?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I need to take this.

choices.text: (Watch him leave. This is my chance to search the office.)

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Excuse me — my lawyer. I need to take this in the hallway.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Ashworth pulls out his phone and steps out, closing the door behind him. He didn't lock it. And he left everything on his desk. I don't have long — he could come back any second. What can I find before he returns?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: How am I — I was poisoned. How do you think I'm feeling? My head feels like someone filled it with cement. My stomach hasn't forgiven me. And I'm stuck in this office because the hospital discharged me too early because — and I quote — 'the poisoning wasn't severe enough to warrant continued observation.'

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Not severe enough. I collapsed in front of three witnesses. My blood pressure was 180 over 'we should probably do something about that.' But sure, not severe enough.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Someone did try to hurt you, Mr. Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Hurt me? They tried to kill me! Or — well, the doctors say the dose was 'non-lethal.' As if that's supposed to make me feel better. 'Congratulations, Mr. Ashworth, someone poisoned you just a little bit.' Wonderful. I feel so much better.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Do you know what really bothers me? Whoever did it knew exactly how much to use. That's not random. That's someone who wanted me alive but out of commission. Which means this isn't about me. It's about this building. Everything is always about this stupid building.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Why am I — because it's mine? Because I bought it with my own money? Because the structural report says it needs four million dollars in repairs that nobody is volunteering to pay?

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Everyone acts like I'm some monster for wanting to tear down a building. You know what they don't tell you in the brochure? The roof leaks. The foundation has cracks. The electrical system is a fire hazard that would make any inspector weep. The plumbing dates back to an era when 'indoor plumbing' was considered cutting-edge technology.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That doesn't mean—

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I know. I know what it 'means.' It means history. Culture. Heritage. I've heard the speech. Edwin's given it to me forty-seven times. Vivian's given it to me in two languages. I get it. But sentiment doesn't pay for earthquake retrofitting. Nostalgia doesn't fix a gas line from 1924.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I'm not the villain here, Drew. I'm just the guy holding the bill.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Enemies? Ha. Take a number. Stand in line.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Vivian looks at me like I'm something she found on the bottom of her shoe. Edwin talks about me the way people talk about war criminals. Stella — actually, Stella is professional about it, I'll give her that. She just quietly hates me with impeccable time management.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Diego is the only one who doesn't seem to actively despise me, which is probably because he's too busy scribbling in his notebook to notice I exist. I caught him taking notes during my meeting with the contractor. Notes! Like I'm a character in his little play.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You don't seem very concerned about being surrounded by people who dislike you.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: I'm a real estate developer, Ms. Drew. If I worried about being liked, I'd have chosen a different career. Maybe something universally beloved, like dentistry or parking enforcement.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: Finally. Go. Investigate. Find whoever poisoned me so I can sue them. And close the door on your way out — there's a draft from the hallway that's been bothering me for forty-five minutes and I refuse to get up because my legs still feel like they're made of pudding.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Still here, ma chère? Go, go — explore. The theater won't investigate itself. I'll be right here when you need me.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Nancy, ma chère. You have that look again — the furrow. Go, go. The theater will keep its secrets whether I'm talking or not. I'll be here, as always.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: And remember — crackers in the concession stand. Only slightly stale.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: You've met him. You see now — the man treats this theater like a balance sheet. Seventy-two hours, he says, as if history has an expiration date.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: He gave me a few leads. But I need more perspective on this place — and on Margaux.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Then go upstairs, chérie. Use the master key — the grand staircase will take you to the auditorium. Edwin Hale has been camped out up there for weeks. He's the theater's historian, self-appointed guardian of every dusty secret in this building.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: If anyone knows what Ashworth is really after — and what this theater is hiding — it's Edwin. And while you're up there, you may run into Stella and Diego. They've been... restless tonight.

lines.speaker: Vivian
lines.text: Be careful, Nancy. This theater has a way of showing you things you weren't looking for.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I've told you everything I know, Nancy. Everything I'm — well. Everything I can. If you need architectural details or historical records, I'm your man. Otherwise, I should get back to my notes. Chapter twelve won't revise itself.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You have a look in your eye that suggests you already know more than I'd like. That's... unsettling. In a professional sense.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I keep checking my phone. Wondering if Edwin's going to text me something terrible. He doesn't even have my number. That's how rattled I am — I'm afraid of a text from a man who communicates exclusively through handwritten index cards.

choices.text: Are you okay, Stella?
I'll handle Edwin. Stay here.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Am I okay. You know, nobody's asked me that since this started? Ashworth asked if I tampered with his tea. Edwin asked if I'd seen his notebook. Vivian asked if I'd wound the lobby clock. Nobody asked if I was okay.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I'm not, for the record. I just told you a man I respect is living in the basement faking a ghost, and now you're going to go confront him and I'm going to stand here pretending to adjust a batten and trying not to throw up.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You did the right thing.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: The right thing. Sure. That's what people say when the thing you did is going to keep you up at 3 AM for the next six months. Go, Drew. Before I change my mind about all of it.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Where else would I go? I've been backstage for eleven years. I know every creak in this building, every draft, every weird smell from the fly gallery. This is the only place I make sense.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Just... when you talk to him... remember he's not a bad person. He's a historian who got desperate. There's a difference. I think.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: Nancy! I'd love to chat but I'm in a really good writing flow right now. The words are just — *chef's kiss* — they're happening. If I stop now, I'll lose the thread and spend three hours staring at a blinking cursor.

lines.speaker: Diego
lines.text: But hey — when you solve this whole thing? I want details. For the play. Strictly for artistic purposes.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: We're done, Drew. I've answered your questions. I've shown you my office. I've been more cooperative than any man who was recently poisoned should have to be.

lines.speaker: Ashworth
lines.text: The demolition is in seventy-two hours. Either find the poisoner by then or get out of my way. Preferably both.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Nancy. I... I suppose I knew this moment would come.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You've found it all, haven't you. The fog machine controls. The trapdoor mechanism. The costume.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The Ghost Project, Edwin. Your notebook is very thorough.

choices.text: You staged the haunting. All of it.
Tell me about the poisoning.
Why, Edwin? Why go this far?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Yes. All of it. The fog. The lights. The figure on stage. The whispers in the walls. I spent four months planning it — every cue, every timing, every sightline.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: The ghost of Margaux Fontaine. My greatest production. And I'm not even a theater person — I'm a historian. But I learned. I practiced at 3 AM when nobody was here. I ran the sequence forty-seven times before the first public performance.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You scared people, Edwin. The crew quit. Ashworth—

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Ashworth was going to destroy this building! Every passage, every secret, every piece of evidence that Margaux was murdered — buried under condominiums. I couldn't let that happen. Not after what my grandfather—

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I made a promise. To a man who spent forty-three years fighting for the truth. I was twelve years old and I promised him I'd finish what he started. So yes. I faked a ghost. I would do it again.

choices.text: And the poisoning? Was that part of the plan too?
I believe you about the ghost. But I need to know about Ashworth.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Poison? I would never — that's not — Nancy, I was trying to SAVE this place, not hurt anyone!

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Look at these bottles. Look at them! They're homemade. I mixed fog fluid and stage effects compounds. I couldn't poison someone if I tried — I'm a historian, not a chemist!

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: The poison used on Ashworth was pharmaceutical grade antimony. Commercially sourced. Your chemicals are crude and hand-mixed.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Because they ARE crude! I followed recipes from a 1920s stage effects manual! The most dangerous thing in these bottles is the glycol fog fluid, and that just makes you cough!

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Edwin, I need you to look me in the eye. Did you poison Roland Ashworth?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: No. God, no. I staged a ghost to save a theater. I trespassed. I used equipment that wasn't mine. I scared people. I lied — I lied to you, to Vivian, to everyone. And I'm sorry for all of it.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: But I did not poison that man. I would never. That's not — that's not who I am.

choices.text: I believe you, Edwin.
Then who did?
Tell me about Cecilia's letter.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I don't know. I genuinely don't know. But Nancy — I was here that night. I was in the basement when Ashworth collapsed.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I heard him fall. Through the ceiling — a thud, then silence. And I was terrified. Not because he was hurt, but because I thought they'd blame me. So I stayed down here. Like a coward.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Did you see anyone else near the office that night?

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: No. But here's the thing that's been eating at me. Ashworth's tea. He makes it the same way every night — Earl Grey, two sugars, from the kettle in his office. Nobody brings it to him. Nobody touches that kettle except him.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You're saying nobody had access to poison his tea.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I'm saying the only person who had regular, unsupervised access to that kettle was Ashworth himself. Make of that what you will.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Edwin, the ghost staging — that's trespassing, property damage, possibly fraud. You know that.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I know. I've known since the first night. Every time I ran the sequence, I knew I was breaking the law. And every time, I told myself it was worth it.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Was it? I still don't know. But Margaux Fontaine was murdered on this stage ninety-seven years ago, and nobody ever answered for it. If my ghost made one person ask questions — if it bought this building one more day — then maybe it was worth it.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I'll figure out the poisoning, Edwin. But you need to stay here until I do.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I'm not going anywhere. I've been in this building for fifteen years. A few more hours won't hurt.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: You found it. Cecilia Drake's confession. The letter she wrote and never sent.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: She confessed to switching the prop goblet for a real one. She put poison in the wine for the Act III death scene. And when Margaux drank from it on stage...

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: My grandfather was in the wings. He watched her fall. He caught her. And she died in his arms.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I found that letter three months ago, hidden in a wall cavity in the passage between the basement and the dressing room. It's the proof my grandfather spent his life looking for.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: This is enough for the landmark petition, Edwin. This is documented proof of a historical crime.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I know. That's why I couldn't let Ashworth tear down the walls. The letter was IN the walls. If the demolition happened before I found it, it would have been destroyed. Ninety-seven years of truth, ground to dust.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Because this theater contains proof that Margaux Fontaine was murdered. And Ashworth was going to destroy it.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: The hidden passages, the wall cavities, the sealed rooms — they're an archive. An accidental time capsule. Cecilia Drake hid a confession letter inside these walls, and it took me three months of searching to find it.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: If the demolition happens, that letter is destroyed. The passages collapse. Every piece of evidence gets bulldozed into rubble.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: I couldn't let that happen. Not after my grandfather spent forty-three years fighting for this truth.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: So you staged a ghost to stop the demolition.

lines.speaker: Edwin
lines.text: Ghost sightings trigger historical review. Historical review delays demolition. It was the only plan I had. And it was working — until someone poisoned Ashworth and turned a ghost story into a crime scene.

choices.text: Let's talk about the poisoning.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I pick up the receiver and listen. Just a dial tone. I've called everyone I can think of — Bess, George, Dad, Ned, even the Historical Society. Time to put down the phone and put the pieces together myself.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: So. The great detective returns. Pick your favorite felony — I've got a whole collection.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Theft, fraud, accessory to ghost-related intimidation... I think that last one's a misdemeanor, but I'm not a lawyer.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Stella, I need to talk to you about what happens next.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Right. The part where everything falls apart. I've been rehearsing this scene all morning.

choices.text: Tell me about your mother.
Why didn't you come to me sooner about Edwin?
I need to decide what to put in my report about you.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Mom? She's... she's tough. Tougher than cancer, tougher than me, tougher than a $47,000 medical bill that insurance decided was 'elective.'

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Elective. Like she elected to get sick. Like there was a ballot.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: She doesn't know about the props. She thinks I picked up extra shifts. If she found out I was stealing to pay for her chemo, she'd check herself out of the hospital just to ground me.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: She sounds like someone worth fighting for.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: She's the only thing I've ever been sure about.

choices.text: Why didn't you come to me sooner about Edwin?
I need to decide what to put in my report about you.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Because I thought the ghost plan would work. Stupid, right? Edwin was so convinced — scare Ashworth, save the theater, everybody wins.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: And I needed the theater to stay open. No theater, no job. No job, no money for Mom.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: By the time I realized Edwin was in over his head, I was in over mine. Funny how that works.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: You could have trusted me.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I don't trust easy. Occupational hazard when your whole life is held together with duct tape and denial.

choices.text: Tell me about your mother.
I need to decide what to put in my report about you.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: I need to decide what to put in my report about you, Stella.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Yeah. I figured this was coming.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Just — whatever you decide. Thanks for listening to the whole story first. Most people don't bother.

choices.text: The props were going to be destroyed anyway. I'm leaving them out.
I have to include everything. But I'll make sure the context is clear.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You — you're serious? You'd do that?

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I'm not going to cry. I'm not. I'm just — the lighting in here is terrible and my eyes are doing a thing.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Stella.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: I'll pay it back. Every cent. I don't know how yet, but I will. I'll even be nice to the actors.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Don't make promises you can't keep.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Fair. I'll pay it back and I'll be slightly less terrible to the actors. Final offer.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: ...Yeah. Okay. I knew you would.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: And honestly? I think that's the right call. I stole things. There should be a record of that.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Just — the context. You said you'd include the context.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: Every word. Your mother, the bills, the demolition. All of it.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Then that's enough. That's more than I expected from anyone.

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: You know what's funny? I feel lighter. Like I've been holding my breath for six months and someone finally said 'exhale.'

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Hey, Nancy? Whatever happens with the theater, with Edwin, with all of it...

lines.speaker: Stella
lines.text: Thanks for seeing me. Not the stage manager, not the thief. Me.

lines.speaker: Nancy
lines.text: That's the job, Stella. Seeing people.
